title: Concur: Proactive Agent-Level Admission Control for Efficient Agentic Batch Inference
core_contribution: Concur reframes GPU KV-cache as a shared finite resource and ports TCP AIMD congestion control to agent-level admission, using KV usage + hit-rate dual-signal feedback to eliminate middle-phase thrashing in agentic batch inference—delivering up to 4.09x throughput on Qwen3-32B and 1.90x on DeepSeek-V3 without engine modification.
summary: Agentic batch inference (RL rollouts, data distillation, agent-as-judge) stresses GPU KV-cache in a sustained, cumulative manner because ReAct-style agents grow their context monotonically and pause asynchronously for tool calls. The authors identify and characterize 'middle-phase thrashing': a three-phase execution pattern (warmup -> thrashing -> cooldown) where KV usage stays near saturation but hit rate collapses, causing ~49% of end-to-end latency to be wasted on eviction-induced recomputation. Existing fixes fail: smarter eviction still targets idle-but-live prefixes; CPU offloading (HiCache) saturates PCIe under concurrency (can be 3x slower than baseline); request-level admission lacks visibility into agent-cumulative cache footprint and can make things worse (0.72x). Concur introduces a lightweight middleware between agent frameworks and the LLM serving engine (SGLang), exposing admit/pause/resume primitives on agent lifecycles. It ports TCP AIMD control with a conjunctive congestion signal U_t > U_high AND H_t < H_thresh, and sustains high throughput with fixed parameters (alpha=2, beta=0.5, U_low=0.2, U_high=0.5). Evaluation on H100 clusters shows 6/6 wins across Qwen3-32B and DeepSeek-V3 configurations; KV hit rate stays at 73-96% where SGLang collapses to 35%; the method beats both static admission and HiCache offloading consistently.
key_findings: Middle-phase thrashing is a distinct performance pathology from classical memory thrashing: memory stays full but hit rate collapses; dominates >90% of execution time and 49% of latency as recomputation overhead.
Request-level admission control can be WORSE than no control (0.72x on Qwen3-32B/TP8) because it lacks agent-cumulative cache visibility.
CPU offloading (HiCache) fails under concurrency due to PCIe contention: achieves 97% hit rate but up to 0.34x speed on DeepSeek-V3/B16.
A dual-signal conjunctive trigger (usage AND hit-rate) is the core engineering insight—usage alone misclassifies healthy high-load as congestion, causing 4-5x latency regression.
AIMD with TCP default parameters (alpha=2, beta=0.5) generalizes across models and workloads; only U_low needs careful calibration.
Static admission limits cannot match adaptive control because workload phase changes (1.5-2.9x gap between best static and Concur).
limitations: Offline batch only; no online/SLO (TTFT, TPOT) evaluation.
Assumes ReAct-style agents with step boundaries; streaming and multi-agent communication not addressed.
Hit-rate signal assumes workload with baseline shared prefixes; may fail on high-diversity workloads where hit rate is always low.
Not integrated with P-D disaggregation, speculative decoding, or multi-engine deployments.
Heterogeneous agent cohorts (mix of short and long agents) may suffer starvation under a single W_t window.
No open-source release announced; no comparison to concurrent agent-native systems (TokenCake, Continuum, Kairos, Autellix).

infra_impact.algorithm: Enables larger effective batch sizes for agentic RL rollouts, directly speeding up policy training; removes a throughput cliff that forced under-batching.
infra_impact.kernel: N/A — pure control plane; uses existing paged-attention / prefix-cache kernels unchanged, but lets them operate under a stable bounded footprint.
infra_impact.framework: Defines a new middleware layer (Agent-Level Controller) between agent frameworks and engines; generalizes request-level admission into agent-level admission with feedback control.
infra_impact.llm: No model architecture changes; validated on dense (Qwen3-32B) and MoE (DeepSeek-V3); models with larger per-token KV (e.g., non-MLA) benefit more.
infra_impact.agent: Core contribution layer—makes ReAct agents first-class scheduling units with pause/resume primitives naturally aligned to tool-call boundaries.
infra_impact.cluster: Indirect—eliminates thrashing-induced wasted GPU-hours, important for large-scale RL training clusters.
infra_impact.code: No open-source release disclosed in paper; implementation is a Python/C++ middleware layered on SGLang.

open_questions: How does Concur behave on heterogeneous agent cohorts (short + long agents mixed)? Does the single W_t cause starvation of long agents?
Can AIMD hyperparameters be learned online, or does the fixed (0.2, 0.5) setting truly generalize across diverse tasks (code, math, browsing)?
How should admission control interact with P-D disaggregation, speculative decoding, or multi-engine serving?
Under adversarial / divergent-prompt workloads where hit-rate never exceeds ~30%, does the controller get stuck in a permanent low-window regime?
How should multi-tenant QoS and priority agents integrate with agent-level admission control?
